questions.question: Explain the difference between propositional logic and first-order logic, focusing on syntax (atoms, quantifiers, predicates) and semantics (interpretations, models).
keyPoints: Propositional logic works on propositions (true/false) with connectives
First-order logic adds variables, predicates, and quantifiers ∀, ∃
Semantics: propositional assignments vs. interpretations with a domain
Quantifiers allow statements about all or some elements of the domain

questions.question: State the inference rules Modus Ponens and Universal Instantiation, and illustrate each with a simple example in propositional and first-order logic respectively.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: from P → Q and P infer Q
Universal Instantiation: from ∀x φ(x) infer φ(c) for any constant c
Example in PL: (P → Q) ∧ P ⇒ Q
Example in FOL: ∀x Human(x) → Mortal(x), Human(Socrates) ⇒ Mortal(Socrates)

questions.question: Given the premises (1) ∀x (Animal(x) → Loves(x, human)), (2) ∃y (Dog(y) ∧ Animal(y)), use first-order inference to derive ∃y (Dog(y) ∧ Loves(y, human)). Show the step-by-step derivation, naming the inference rules used.
keyPoints: Apply Existential Instantiation to (2) to obtain Dog(a) ∧ Animal(a)
Apply Modus Ponens with (1) to infer Animal(a) → Loves(a, human)
From Dog(a) ∧ Animal(a) derive Animal(a) via And-Elimination
Apply Modus Ponens to get Loves(a, human) then Existential Generalization to obtain ∃y (Dog(y) ∧ Loves(y, human))

questions.question: Prove the equivalence of the propositional formula ¬(P ∨ Q) ↔ (¬P ∧ ¬Q) using (a) logical equivalence laws and (b) a truth table. Then convert the left-hand side to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF).
keyPoints: Apply De Morgan's law: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q, establishing equivalence
Truth table: compare columns for ¬(P ∨ Q) and (¬P ∧ ¬Q) – identical truth values
CNF: ¬(P ∨ Q) is already a conjunction of unit clauses (¬P) ∧ (¬Q)
DNF: the formula is a single conjunction term (¬P ∧ ¬Q), which is also its DNF
